card.indications: Treatment of acute manic episodes and maintenance treatment of bipolar disorder. It may also be used in selected patients as augmentation for treatment-resistant depression.
card.mechanism: Modifies intracellular signaling and neurotransmitter activity, including effects on second-messenger systems, serotonin, dopamine, norepinephrine, and glutamate. The exact mood-stabilizing mechanism is not fully defined.
card.dosing: Dose is individualized to clinical response and serum concentration. Acute mania often requires divided dosing with higher target levels than maintenance therapy. Renal impairment and older age require lower doses and closer monitoring. Extended-release products must not be crushed.
card.routeAdministration: Give with food or milk if GI upset occurs and at the same times each day. Maintain consistent sodium and fluid intake. Draw serum levels at the correct post-dose interval, usually as a trough, and recheck after dose changes, interacting medications, dehydration, or illness.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to product components. Avoid or use only with specialist oversight in severe renal or cardiovascular disease, severe dehydration or sodium depletion, and pregnancy when risks outweigh benefits.
card.warnings: Boxed warning: lithium toxicity is closely related to serum concentration and can occur near therapeutic levels. Dehydration, low sodium, renal impairment, fever, vomiting, diarrhea, heavy sweating, and interacting drugs can rapidly increase concentrations. Other risks include nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, chronic kidney injury, hypothyroidism, hyperparathyroidism, arrhythmias, and fetal harm.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Fine tremor, nausea, diarrhea, thirst, frequent urination, mild cognitive slowing, fatigue, weight gain, acne, and edema.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Lithium toxicity with coarse tremor, ataxia, confusion, slurred speech, seizures, coma, arrhythmias, renal failure, nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, severe hypothyroidism, hypercalcemia, and serotonin syndrome.
card.interactions: Thiazide and loop diuretics, ACE inhibitors, ARBs, NSAIDs, metronidazole, and dehydration can increase lithium concentrations. Caffeine and high sodium intake may lower levels. Serotonergic drugs increase serotonin-syndrome risk.
card.monitoring: Monitor serum lithium concentrations, renal function, sodium, thyroid function, calcium, weight, fluid balance, pregnancy status when relevant, ECG in patients with cardiac risk, mood response, tremor, gait, and signs of toxicity.
card.patientTeaching: Keep salt and fluid intake consistent and avoid dehydration. Call promptly for vomiting, diarrhea, fever, heavy sweating, new medications, worsening tremor, unsteady gait, slurred speech, confusion, or severe drowsiness. Do not double missed doses or use NSAIDs without checking first.
card.nutrition: Maintain steady sodium intake and drink adequate fluids unless restricted. Avoid crash diets, severe sodium restriction, and sudden major changes in caffeine use. Alcohol can worsen dehydration and sedation.

card.indications: Symptomatic control of acute nonspecific diarrhea and chronic diarrhea associated with inflammatory bowel disease or other conditions when infectious or inflammatory causes requiring different treatment have been excluded.
card.mechanism: Activates peripheral mu-opioid receptors in the intestinal wall, reducing propulsive motility, increasing transit time, and improving fluid and electrolyte absorption.
card.dosing: Adult prescription dosing for acute diarrhea commonly starts with 4 mg, then 2 mg after each unformed stool, within the prescribed daily maximum. Chronic diarrhea is individualized. Pediatric use is age and weight based. Do not exceed labeled limits.
card.routeAdministration: Give after loose stools as directed and stop when stools normalize or if constipation or abdominal distention develops. Replace fluids and electrolytes. Avoid use when bloody diarrhea, high fever, severe abdominal pain, or suspected C. difficile or invasive bacterial infection is present unless specifically ordered.
card.contraindications: Children younger than 2 years, hypersensitivity, acute dysentery with blood and high fever, acute ulcerative colitis, bacterial enterocolitis from invasive organisms, C. difficile-associated diarrhea, and situations where slowing intestinal movement should be avoided.
card.warnings: Doses above recommendations can cause QT prolongation, torsades de pointes, ventricular arrhythmias, cardiac arrest, syncope, and death. Toxic megacolon, ileus, and serious CNS or respiratory depression can occur, especially in young children or overdose.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Constipation, abdominal cramping, nausea, dizziness, dry mouth, and flatulence.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Torsades de pointes, cardiac arrest, ileus, toxic megacolon, severe abdominal distention, anaphylaxis, severe skin reactions, and CNS or respiratory depression in overdose.
card.interactions: Strong CYP3A4, CYP2C8, or P-glycoprotein inhibitors can increase exposure, especially at high doses. Other QT-prolonging drugs may increase arrhythmia risk. CNS depressants worsen sedation in overdose.
card.monitoring: Monitor stool frequency and character, hydration, electrolytes when diarrhea is significant, abdominal distention, bowel sounds, fever, blood in stool, cardiac symptoms, and duration of therapy.
card.patientTeaching: Do not take more than directed. Stop and seek care for abdominal swelling, severe pain, bloody stool, fever, fainting, palpitations, or diarrhea lasting more than 48 hours without improvement. Continue oral fluids and electrolytes.
card.nutrition: Use oral rehydration fluids and a tolerated diet. Avoid foods or drinks that worsen diarrhea. This medicine does not replace fluid and electrolyte treatment.

card.indications: Treatment of hypertension, reduction of stroke risk in selected patients with hypertension and left ventricular hypertrophy, and treatment of diabetic nephropathy in type 2 diabetes with hypertension and proteinuria.
card.mechanism: Selectively blocks angiotensin II at AT1 receptors, reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone secretion and lowering blood pressure without inhibiting bradykinin breakdown.
card.dosing: The usual adult starting dose for hypertension is 50 mg once daily, with 25 mg used in selected volume-depleted or hepatically impaired patients. The dose may be increased to 100 mg daily based on response.
card.routeAdministration: Give once daily with or without food. Check blood pressure, potassium, and renal function before and during therapy. Correct significant volume depletion when possible. Stop promptly if pregnancy occurs.
card.contraindications: Pregnancy, hypersensitivity, and concurrent aliskiren use in patients with diabetes.
card.warnings: Boxed warning: fetal toxicity. Other risks include hypotension, hyperkalemia, acute kidney injury, worsening renal function in renal artery stenosis, and rare angioedema.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Dizziness, fatigue, nasal congestion, back pain, hypotension, increased potassium, and increased creatinine.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Fetal injury or death, severe hypotension, acute kidney injury, life-threatening hyperkalemia, angioedema, and severe allergic reactions.
card.interactions: Potassium supplements, potassium-sparing diuretics, salt substitutes, and trimethoprim increase hyperkalemia risk. NSAIDs can reduce antihypertensive effect and worsen renal function. Lithium concentrations may increase.
card.monitoring: Monitor blood pressure, renal function, potassium, volume status, edema, signs of angioedema, and pregnancy status when relevant.
card.patientTeaching: Do not use during pregnancy. Rise slowly, keep laboratory appointments, and report facial or tongue swelling, fainting, decreased urine, or muscle weakness. Avoid potassium salt substitutes unless approved.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Follow sodium guidance and avoid unapproved potassium supplements or potassium-containing salt substitutes.

card.indications: Adjunct to diet and lifestyle measures for treatment of primary hypercholesterolemia and mixed dyslipidemia and for reduction of cardiovascular risk in selected patients.
card.mechanism: Competitively inhibits HMG-CoA reductase, decreasing hepatic cholesterol synthesis and increasing LDL-receptor activity, which lowers LDL cholesterol and triglycerides and modestly raises HDL cholesterol.
card.dosing: Immediate-release lovastatin is commonly started at 20 mg once daily with the evening meal, with lower starting doses in selected patients. Extended-release products are generally taken at bedtime without food. Dose limits apply with interacting drugs.
card.routeAdministration: Give immediate-release tablets with the evening meal. Give extended-release tablets at bedtime and swallow whole. Review all medications for CYP3A4 interactions before starting or increasing the dose.
card.contraindications: Active liver disease or unexplained persistent transaminase elevations, pregnancy, breastfeeding, and concurrent use with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors such as clarithromycin, itraconazole, or certain HIV protease inhibitors.
card.warnings: Can cause myopathy and rhabdomyolysis, especially with high exposure, older age, renal impairment, hypothyroidism, or interacting drugs. Hepatic injury and increased glucose may occur.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Headache, abdominal discomfort, constipation, nausea, muscle aches, and mild liver-enzyme elevation.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Myopathy, rhabdomyolysis with acute kidney injury, immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy, severe hepatic injury, anaphylaxis, and serious drug interactions.
card.interactions: Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors are contraindicated. Gemfibrozil, cyclosporine, danazol, amiodarone, verapamil, diltiazem, dronedarone, colchicine, and large amounts of grapefruit can increase myopathy risk. Warfarin effect may change.
card.monitoring: Monitor fasting lipid response, liver tests when clinically indicated, muscle pain or weakness, creatine kinase when symptoms occur, renal function if rhabdomyolysis is suspected, and medication interactions.
card.patientTeaching: Report unexplained muscle pain, tenderness, weakness, dark urine, severe fatigue, or jaundice. Avoid pregnancy and tell every clinician about lovastatin before receiving antibiotics or antifungals.
card.nutrition: Continue a cholesterol-lowering diet. Take immediate-release tablets with the evening meal. Avoid large quantities of grapefruit or grapefruit juice and limit alcohol.

card.indications: Treatment of acute magnesium deficiency, prevention and control of seizures in severe preeclampsia or eclampsia, and selected treatment of torsades de pointes or other protocol-defined conditions.
card.mechanism: Replaces magnesium, stabilizes excitable membranes, reduces acetylcholine release at neuromuscular junctions, and decreases CNS and vascular excitability.
card.dosing: Dose depends on indication, serum magnesium, renal function, and protocol. Obstetric regimens commonly use a 4- to 6-g IV loading dose followed by 1 to 2 g/hour. Severe hypomagnesemia and arrhythmia dosing are individualized. Reduce doses in renal impairment.
card.routeAdministration: Dilute concentrated solutions before IV administration. Use an infusion pump and monitor closely. For obstetric therapy, assess respirations, reflexes, urine output, and fetal status. Keep calcium gluconate available as an antidote for toxicity.
card.contraindications: Heart block or significant myocardial damage. Use extreme caution with renal impairment, hypermagnesemia, and neuromuscular disease.
card.warnings: Excess magnesium can cause loss of deep-tendon reflexes, respiratory paralysis, hypotension, heart block, cardiac arrest, and death. Prolonged use in pregnancy beyond several days can cause fetal bone abnormalities and neonatal hypocalcemia.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Flushing, warmth, sweating, nausea, lethargy, muscle weakness, blurred vision, and infusion-site discomfort.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Respiratory depression or arrest, severe hypotension, loss of reflexes, heart block, cardiac arrest, pulmonary edema, and fetal or neonatal toxicity with prolonged exposure.
card.interactions: Other CNS depressants, opioids, sedatives, and neuromuscular blockers increase respiratory or neuromuscular depression. Calcium-channel blockers may increase hypotension or weakness.
card.monitoring: Monitor respiratory rate, oxygenation, blood pressure, pulse, ECG when indicated, deep-tendon reflexes, urine output, serum magnesium, calcium, renal function, mental status, and fetal status during obstetric use.
card.patientTeaching: Explain flushing and warmth may occur. Report trouble breathing, chest pain, extreme weakness, or decreased urine immediately. Obstetric patients should understand the reason for continuous monitoring.
card.nutrition: No specific food restriction during acute IV therapy. Maintain fluids as ordered and avoid unapproved magnesium-containing antacids, laxatives, or supplements.
